results.possibleIdentity: Malvin Carl Teich is an American electrical engineer, physicist, and computational neuroscientist. Born on May 4, 1939, in New York City, he is currently a Professor Emeritus at both Columbia University and Boston University. He joined the faculty of Columbia University in September 1967, where he served in the Department of Electrical Engineering (acting as Chairman from 1978 to 1980) and the Department of Applied Physics and Applied Mathematics. During the late 1960s and early 1970s, Teich was a specialist in quantum electronics and lasers, fields in which he made significant contributions, including the first observation of the two-photon photoelectric effect in metallic sodium (his 1966 Ph.D. dissertation at Cornell University) and the demonstration of optimal heterodyne detection in the middle-infrared region using CO2 lasers. Teich is widely recognized as the co-author of the seminal textbook 'Fundamentals of Photonics' (with Bahaa E. A. Saleh). His career also expanded into computational neuroscience, focusing on fractal stochastic processes in neural information transmission. He was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization as a technical professional available for collaborations with artists, as evidenced by E.A.T. registry records from the late 1960s which listed his affiliation with Columbia University and expertise in quantum electronics and lasers.
